Aisha Chowry the chief commercial officer of Infinium. Let's go straight to our last my third and last interview from your aviation day. This is the director general of airports council international world ACI world Justin Arbachi. What is ACI world? We have 19 airports who have already achieved level five. So airports are mainly focused in scope one and two areas of of carbon neutrality and that's where they can control what they do at the airport by electrifying their ground equipment by building sustainable solutions by making a more efficient heating and cooling systems. So there are a number of things that airports can do to address scope one and two carbon uh goals. Now, when it comes to scope 3, that's where really you need the the airlines to do a lot of work there. But airports are doing a lot to help to put in infrastructure that allows the use of staff and other alternative fuels and working very closely with the airline counterparts to try to put things in place to make the airport as sustainable as possible. The use of sustainable aviation fuel of itself is not complex. It is the production of sustainable aviation fuel that is the problem. It's not so much of a technology problem as is a financial problem. The manufacturers are ready to make sustainable aviation fuel and are eager to do so, but they need the commitment of the airlines and the industry to purchase that so that they know that they can get a return on their investment. And that's a little bit challenging because airlines and others are somewhat hesitant because of the fluctuation of the pricing and the uncertainty of pricing in the future to make long-term commitments. the other alternative aviation fuels, electric aircraft, hydrogen aircraft, the technology is not there yet. And our commitment has to be unwavering. Justin Erbachi is director general of airports council international world lia where can people find out more about the work you're doing the world economic forum we're moving this agenda forward trying to decaponize the aviation sector we all want to continue flying but we want to do so in a sustainable manner so tune into what we're doing at airports of tomorrow you'll find our website I'll put the link in the show notes la head of climate strategy net0ero at the world economic forum thanks for joining us on Radio Dallas. Thanks, Robin. And thanks to all our guests today. Paul Griffith, CEO of Dubai Airports, Aesa Chowry, chief commercial officer at Infinium, and Justin Ebachi, director general of Airports Council International World. title: Climate science is clearer than ever. How should companies respond?
transcript: 2024 we for the first time experienced 1.5° CC. It cost the global economy over $200 billion US in all the droughts, floods, heat waves, fires, disease outbreaks. Welcome to Radio Davos, the podcast from the World Economic Forum that looks at the biggest challenges and how we might tackle them. This week we're returning to one of the biggest challenges, climate change. what we're seeing at the global level with ambiguity in the policy space and sort of a retreat on ambitious decarbonization policies. But at the same time, we're seeing the increased physical impact of climate. >> If a large part of some of the biggest economies of the world start looking in some other direction and start only focusing on profit maximization but not looking at the carbon side at all and climate change at all, then that's obviously going to weaken the whole effort. A science aligned pathway is is the winning pathway for the future. >> This is a massive massive business opportunity for companies that choose to change their own pathway going forward. This can be actually a big differentiator for them. >> In this episode, we have two great guests to talk about climate science, climate policy, and the business response to all of that. And I'm joined by a co-host this week, my colleague Pim Valdry. Pim, how are you? I'm very good. Thank you. >> What do you do at the World Economic Forum? >> So, uh I head uh or co-head our climate work and in that role I have the pleasure to also lead the secretariat of the alliance of sea climate leaders which is a global coalition of 130 companies across 12 sectors and 27 countries and we we essentially do three things uh with these companies. We help them accelerate emission reduction and mitigation action. Really looking at the the value chain emission reduction which are the sort of notorious reduction that can stand for about 85% of corporate emission um uh GSG emissions. We also work to increase business leadership on climate resilience and adaptation and how do we embed climate risk into corporate decision- making and strategy to make sure that we deliver value not only to shareholders but to society as a whole. And thirdly, we catalyze public private collaboration. Many of the knock-on effects of the climate crisis if you look at food price uh inflation, higher insurance premiums or infrastructure upgrades that will be needed is a public private problem and challenge and it needs a public private solution rooted in science and that's why we're so thrilled today really to introduce two of our speakers. Uh the first is professor Yuan Rostrom who's the director of the potam institute for climate impact research who's pioneered the concept of planetary boundaries framework. We'll talk a little bit uh to you about the state of science and also Susum Cena who's the chairman and chief executive officer of renew and one of India's largest renewable energy companies and he's also the co-chair of the ELISO climate leaders. I think over to you Robin to kick us off. >> Thanks Pim. Great to meet you both. Let's start with the science. We have Yan Rostrom. Tell us where does the science stand now. We've been thinking about climate change for a couple of generations now. This is not a new thing. And isn't it interesting to hear there are still climate skeptics. Is it still in the balance whether climate change is happening, whether humans are responsible for it happening and what the uh remedies need to be. What do we know from science right now? >> Yeah, thank thanks Robin and great to be with you. Good to see you pim pim as well. And u well science is standing more firmly than ever before. Um remember that climate science has u more than a century under its belt since in the late 19th century postulated the first calculations of how greenhouse gases warm the planet because of uh returning or the energy imbalance caused by more heat being trapped under the greenhouse gases than is released back into space. And this is now since many decades so well established that I would argue that it's um um as established as um the fact that we all accept that um if you drop your mobile phone uh it falls to the floor because of something called gravity. So, so it's as firmly and and evidence-based unequivocally established that greenhouse gases causes global warming which impacts on climate triggering extreme events and shifting the entire heat balance on Earth which changes hydrarology and freshwater supply. It causes heat waves. It changes ecology and it fundamentally undermines what we call life support for humanity or the basis for the global economy namely the stability of the environmental systems that we all depend on everything from sea levels to forest systems to soils. So that is where the science stands. But to your question where we are today, if you just look at the last two, three years, we are unfortunately scientifically forced to um raise our concerns to an even higher level than the basic level that we have been standing on firmly for six IPCC, Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change reports, which is the fundament upon which the entire climate science community stands on, which has led to the conclusion that we have a global climate crisis. But just over the past two three years we see something very worrying from the observations namely a tendency of accelerated warming. So we have a situation where we have been warming sc warning scientifically for decades that more greenhouse gases and fossil fuel burning leads linearly to more temperature rise and we reached uh you know up at close to 1.5° C of global mean surface temperature rise and remember this is we also know today with high degree of certainty the warmest temperature on earth over the past 100,000 years since we left the last last ice age 18,000 years ago. We've never seen not only temperatures at this level that we experience right as we speak. But we've never seen the rate of warming as we see right now, but also over the past decade we see this accelerated rate of warming. Meaning that um unexpectedly the the rate of warming seems to go faster than we had expected.
